## Analysis

My analysis supports my claim that the course should record lectures and post them for students who are not able to/do not attend class because it will help them keep up and succeed in the class. I chose to analyze the categories of ls_effective, add_livestream, pace, understanding, year, and tech_impact. I found the mean of every category to see what the overall trend for these categories were and the evidence showed that the majority of students beleived that livestream lectures and lesson videos would be beneficial with means of approximately 5.46 and 5.19. These are both on the higher end of our response range of 1-7. When it came to pace and understanding these numbers werent as conclusive. While we saw drops when compared to livestream lectures and lesson videos they were still slightly above the midpoint range of 3.5 with approximate means of 4.62 and 4.20. When you look at the mean of technological impact it is also in the higher range at approximately 5.08. While you can find the mean for year it means nothing in our analysis. 

When I graphed these against each other I chose to plot lesson videos versus add livestream, pace versus understanding, and year vs technological impact. I chose a line graph for the first two and a box plot for the last. The first line plot shows that the majority of students beleive that both lesson videos and livestream lectures would be beneficial and that the have a positive correlation showing that as it becomes more beneficial for someone the other does as well. 

The second lineplot shows is interesting as that at first as pace is increasing, understanding increases slightly, but quickly this changes and as pace increases understanding drops. This is a negative correlation which is normal to see because typically as a classes pace increases it is harder to understand. 

The last visual is a boxplot that shows how technological impact effects each year. Boxplots are read by putting fifty percent of the data inside of the box and the upper and lower twenty-five percent on the 'whiskers'. This plot shows that as students become younger i.e. the graduation year increases the upper fifty percent of data rises. This shows a trend that as students age decreases, technology impacts their lives more. 

All of this data supports my claim. There is a general want for lesson videos and livestream lectures showing that recording the lectures would be beneficial to most students. While most of the data in the second plot shows that most of the data is in the beginning increase, it also shows that as pace increases, understanding decreases. This implies that recording lectures would be beneficial so that students can rewatch what was taught. The third plot shows that this trend will continue. Technological impact is increases in the younger generation implying that more and more students will want some form of online lecture. 

This idea can be as simple as recording lectures and posting them online. While I am not sure of the infrastruture of UNC I know that most professors do this and post it on Panopto. While there could be a cost to the Department of Computer Science, UNC already implements this software so the cost should be minimal. This will positively impact students that miss class while having zero effect if not a positive one on those that attend lecture because they can rewatch the lecture if they are confused on a topic. This could negatively impact students that run office hours and tutoring but I believe that being able to rewatch the lecture will not replace the benefit of having someone work through problems and code with you. 

What could benefit the analysis of this data is being able to see how confused students are on a topic after a lecture. We could implment the same scoring system that was used for other categories based on a scoring system of 1-7. Overall based on my analysis recording lectures would be an overall benefit and would boost the learning environment in COMP110.
